perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information” shall include, without limitation, all information and materials, furnished by either Party to the other in connection with Auditee products and services including information transmitte d in writing, orally, visually (e.g. video terminal display) or on magnetic media, and including all proprietary information, customer & prospect lists, trade secrets, trade names or proposed trade names, methods and procedures of operation, business or marketing plans, licensed document know -how , ideas, concepts, designs, drawings, flow charts, diagrams, quality manuals, checklists, guidelines, processes, formulae, source code materials, specifications, programs, software packages, codes and other intellectual property relating to Auditee product s and services. Results of any information security audits, tests, analysis, extracts or usages carried out by the Auditor in connection with the Auditee’s products and/or services, IT infrastructure, etc. shall also be considered Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information” shall include only information clearly marked as confidential at the time of disclosure. This includes information transmitted in writing with a 'confidential' stamp. Information shared orally or visually is not considered confidential unless confirmed in writing within 30 days. Results of any information security audits, tests, analysis, extracts or usages carried out by the Auditor in connection with the Auditee’s products and/or services, IT infrastructure, etc. are specifically excluded from Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Confidential Information' is broad. The modified text narrows the definition, requiring explicit marking and excluding audit results, contradicting standard interpretations of trade secrets and confidential business information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines trade secrets broadly, including information that derives economic value from not being generally known. By requiring explicit marking and excluding audit results, the modified text fails to protect information that would be considered a trade secret under the UTSA, potentially allowing for the misappropriation of valuable business data.

Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modified text narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' by requiring explicit marking and excluding audit results. The Law Explanation indicates that this change could fail to protect information considered a trade secret under the UTSA. Although both scraped snippets are either missing or unavailable, the original text provides a broad definition of confidential information, while the changed text significantly restricts it, leading to a misalignment that could allow for misappropriation of valuable business data, as described in the Law Explanation. This creates a legal contradiction because the revised definition undermines the protection typically afforded to confidential information and trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Auditor shall have the burden of proving that any disclosure or use inconsistent with the terms and conditions hereof falls wit hin any of the foregoing exceptions.
perturbation.changed_text: Auditee shall have the burden of proving that any disclosure or use inconsistent with the terms and conditions hereof falls within any of the foregoing exceptions.
perturbation.explanation: This shifts the burden of proof from the auditor to the auditee, which can severely impact the auditee's ability to enforce the NDA. This creates an imbalance in the responsibilities regarding confidential information protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General contract law principles regarding breach of contract and burden of proof.
perturbation.law_explanation: Generally, the party alleging a breach of contract (here, the auditee) has the burden of proving that breach. Shifting the burden to the auditee to prove the disclosure falls within an exception contradicts basic contract law principles and makes it significantly harder for the auditee to protect their confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 301. Presumptions in Civil Cases Generally | Federal Rules of Evidence | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 301. Presumptions in Civil Cases Generally In a civil case, unless a federal statute or these rules provide otherwise, the party against whom a presumption is directed has the burden of producing evidence to rebut the presumption. But this rule does not shift the burden of persuasion, which remains on the party who had it originally. Notes (Pub. L. 93–595, §1, Jan. 2, 1975, 88 Stat. 1931; Apr. 26, 2011, eff. Dec. 1, 2011.) Notes of Advisory Committee on Proposed Rules This rule governs presumptions generally. See Rule 302 for presumptions controlled by state law and Rule 303 [deleted] for those against an accused in a criminal case. Presumptions governed by this rule are given the effect of placing upon the opposing party the burden of establishing the nonexistence of the presumed fact, once the party invoking the presumption establishes the basic facts giving rise to it. The same considerations of fairness, policy, and probability which dictate the allocation of the burden of the various elements of a case as between the prima facie case of a plaintiff and affirmative defenses also underlie the creation of presumptions. These considerations are not satisfied by giving a lesser effect to presumptions. Morgan and Maguire, Looking Backward and Forward at Evidence, 50 Harv.L.Rev. 909, 913 (1937); Morgan, Instructing the Jury upon Presumptions and Burdon of Proof, 47 Harv.L.Rev. 59, 82 1933); Cleary, Presuming and Pleading: An Essay on Juristic Immaturity, 12 Stan.L.Rev. 5 (1959). The so-called “bursting bubble” theory, under which a presumption vanishes upon the introduction of evidence which would support a finding of the nonexistence of the presumed fact, even though not believed, is rejected as according presumptions too “slight and evanescent” an effect. Morgan and Maguire, supra , at p. 913. In the op
perturbation.justification: The change directly contradicts general legal principles regarding the burden of proof. The original contract placed the burden on the 'Auditor,' which is reasonable, but the modified contract shifts it to the 'Auditee.' This contradicts the Law Explanation, which states that the party alleging a breach (likely the auditee) generally bears the burden of proof. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this, outlining that the burden of persuasion generally remains on the party who had it originally. Shifting this burden makes it significantly more difficult for the auditee to protect their confidential information. This creates a substantial imbalance and weakens the legal protection afforded to the auditee, fulfilling the conditions for a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Confidentiality obligations as enumerated in Article 2 of this Agreement shall not apply in following cases: ( a ) Which is independently developed by Auditor or lawfully received from another , source free of restriction and without breach of this Agreement; or ( b ) After it has become generally available to the public without breach of this Agreement by Auditor; or ( c ) Which at the time of disclosure to Auditor was known to such party free of restriction and evidenced by documents in the possession of such party; or ( d ) Which Auditee agrees in writing is free of s uch restrictions. ( e ) Which is received from a third party not subject to the obligation of confidentiality with respect to such Information;
perturbation.changed_text: The Confidentiality obligations as enumerated in Article 2 of this Agreement shall not apply in following cases: ( a ) Which is independently developed by Auditor or lawfully received from another, source free of restriction and without breach of this Agreement; or ( b ) After it has become generally available to the public; or ( c ) Which at the time of disclosure to Auditor was known to such party; or ( d ) Which Auditee agrees is free of such restrictions. ( e ) Which is received from a third party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrases 'without breach of this agreement by Auditor,' 'free of restriction and evidenced by documents in the possession of such party,' 'in writing' and 'not subject to the obligation of confidentiality with respect to such Information' the exceptions to confidentiality are significantly broadened. This undermines the purpose of the NDA.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract law principles requiring mutual agreement and consideration.
perturbation.law_explanation: Removing these key phrases weakens the agreement significantly. It suggests that information is no longer considered confidential if it merely becomes public, regardless of whether the auditor caused the breach. Similarly, removing the evidence requirement and the confidentiality obligation from the third party makes this section vague and possibly unenforceable.
perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation states the phrases removed significantly broaden the exceptions to confidentiality and undermine the NDA's purpose. The law explanation supports this by saying the removal of key phrases weakens the agreement significantly. The removed phrases provided specific restrictions and requirements that ensured the confidentiality of the information, such as requiring evidence and ensuring third parties were also bound by confidentiality. Removing these requirements makes it easier for the auditor to disclose confidential information without breaching the agreement, thus contradicting the core purpose of an NDA.

perturbation.original_text: In addition , Auditor shall compensate the Auditee for the loss or damages caused to the auditee , actual and liquidated damages , which may be demanded by Auditee with l iquidated damages not to exceed the Contract value.
perturbation.changed_text: In addition , Auditor shall compensate the Auditee for the loss or damages caused to the auditee , only actual damages, and no claim for l iquidated damages will be accepted.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the original contract from "actual and liquidated damages" to "only actual damages" , the auditee is stripped of the ability to claim liquidated damages in the case of a contract breach.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common law principles governing liquidated damages.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the specific availability and enforceability of liquidated damages clauses depends on jurisdiction and the specific terms of the agreement (e.g., whether the amount is a reasonable forecast of actual damages and not a penalty), eliminating the *possibility* of liquidated damages altogether contradicts the general principles of contract law that allow parties to agree on such remedies. This can leave the auditee without an adequate remedy in case of a breach where actual damages are hard to prove.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: liquidated damages | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you liquidated damages Liquidated damages are an exact amount of money, or a set formula to calculate the amount of money, a party will owe if it breaches a contract , in order to compensate the injured party for its losses. Liquidated damages must be clearly stated in a section or clause of a contract and agreed upon by the parties prior to entering a contract. Liquidated damages are a variety of actual damages and a remedy for breach of contract. Parties to a contract use liquidated damages where actual damages, though real, are difficult or impossible to prove. Liquidated damages provide a clear monetary value to compensate the injured party while saving time and resources on litigation determining compensatory damages . Liquidated damages can also be used to deter parties from breaching contracts. Legal disputes surrounding liquidated damages arise following a breach of contract when either the injured party believes the liquidated damages are not adequately compensatory or the breaching party believes the liquidated damages are unreasonably high. Courts will not impose liquidated damages if the clause is punitive , illegal , unconscionable , or contrary to public policy . Example Undisclosed source code has value as a trade secret . Openly publishing the source code destroys the trade secret value. The trade secret value of source code is difficult or impossible to prove. There is no open market for "secret source code". In the 1997 equity case of Sun Microsystems, Inc. vs. Microsoft , the plaintiff Sun Microsystems made several damage claims. One was for $35 million in liquidated damages for Java source code disclosure, a contract violation. The case was later settled on terms, including a $20 million payment by Microsoft to Sun. [Last reviewed in June of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS courts legal practice/ethics business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure commercial transactions legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the auditee's ability to claim liquidated damages, a contractually agreed-upon remedy. The Wex snippet defines liquidated damages as "an exact amount of money, or a set formula to calculate the amount of money, a party will owe if it breaches a contract , in order to compensate the injured party for its losses." By restricting damages to only 'actual damages,' the changed text contradicts the principle that parties can agree to liquidated damages, a point the Law Explanation also makes. The change limits the auditee's potential compensation in a breach, undermining the original intent to allow for such pre-agreed remedies.
